title: Electoral Systems: Comparative Analysis of Voting Methods

content.summary: Electoral systems translate votes into seats through various mechanisms including plurality/majoritarian, proportional representation, and mixed systems, each producing distinct political outcomes.
content.body: Three primary electoral system families exist: (1) Plurality/Majoritarian Systems: First-Past-The-Post (FPTP) awards seat to candidate with most votes (UK, US, Canada, India); Two-Round System requires majority, with runoff if no candidate achieves 50%+1 (France presidential elections); Alternative Vote (Ranked Choice) allows ranking candidates, eliminates lowest until majority achieved (Australia House). Characteristics: Tend toward two-party systems (Duverger's Law), single-party governments, strong executive, but may produce disproportionate results (2019 UK: Conservatives 43.6% votes → 56.2% seats). (2) Proportional Representation (PR): Party List PR allocates seats proportionally to vote share using formulas (d'Hondt, Sainte-Laguë); Single Transferable Vote enables ranked choice in multi-member districts (Ireland, Malta); Thresholds typically 3-5% prevent fragmentation. Characteristics: Multi-party systems, coalition governments, better minority representation, but potential instability (Israel, Italy). (3) Mixed Systems: Mixed Member Proportional (MMP) combines FPTP districts with compensatory list seats (Germany, New Zealand); Parallel systems run both separately without compensation (Japan, Russia). Duverger's mechanical effect (seat allocation) and psychological effect (strategic voting) explain party system convergence.
key_points: FPTP produces two-party systems and single-party governments
PR yields multi-party coalitions and proportional outcomes
Duverger's Law: plurality systems favor two-party competition
Electoral thresholds (3-5%) limit party fragmentation in PR
MMP combines local representation with proportionality
No system simultaneously maximizes all democratic values
examples: 2019 UK Election: Conservatives 43.6% votes → 365 seats (56.2%); Lib Dems 11.5% → 11 seats (1.7%)
2021 New Zealand: Labour 50.0% votes → 65 seats (50.8%); Greens 7.9% → 10 seats (7.7%) under MMP
2022 Netherlands: 17 parties won seats; highest vote-getter (VVD) received only 23.6%

sources.citation: Duverger, Maurice. Political Parties: Their Organization and Activity in the Modern State. Wiley, 1954.
Lijphart, Arend. Patterns of Democracy. Yale University Press, 2nd ed., 2012.
UK Electoral Commission. '2019 General Election Results'

temporal_scope.notes: Recent trend toward mixed systems; ranked choice adoption increasing in US municipalities
